summary:
The Chinchorro culture was a hunter-gatherer-fisher society of the hyperarid Atacama Desert coast of northern Chile and southern Peru, with origins in the period around 10,000–9,000 BCE and lasting until approximately 1,500 BCE. Named after the Chinchorro beach in Arica, Chile, where characteristic artifacts were first identified, the culture is renowned for producing the world's oldest known intentionally mummified human remains — artificially preserved bodies predating the Egyptian mummies by approximately 2,000 years.

Chinchorro mummies were not the product of elite or royal burial practices but appear to have been created for entire community members including infants and children — an egalitarian treatment of the dead strikingly different from later mummy-making traditions elsewhere. The earliest mummies date to approximately 5050 BCE, but the cultural tradition from which they emerged had roots in the earlier maritime forager communities of the Atacama coast, established by 10,000 BCE. These communities subsisted on the exceptionally rich cold-water marine resources of the Humboldt Current — fish, shellfish, sea lions, marine birds — that made the desert coast habitable despite the near-total absence of rainfall.

The origins and early phases of the Chinchorro culture from approximately 10,000–8,000 BCE represent the establishment of this long-lived coastal maritime tradition in one of the world's most extreme environments. The Chinchorro demonstrate that complex ritual behavior and sophisticated treatment of the dead could develop among hunter-gatherer peoples in the absence of agriculture, given sufficient marine resource productivity to sustain sedentary or semi-sedentary populations.
